scoring_rule: Unanimous-agreement: a probe matches only if all runs agree AND the agreed answer matches expected. Three probe classes (content / readability / provenance) scored independently; no composite. See docs/failure-mode-catalog.md v0.3 amendments.

probe_results.question: Does the document mention a function named 'binary_search' (or 'binary search'), and does it contain the Python keyword 'def' or 'return' in proximity to that name?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 1 verbatim block defines `def binary_search(arr, target)` with multiple `return` statements. Section 4 Glossary names the function.

probe_results.question: In the document, is the binary_search function presented as a code block — whether via fenced markdown (```), indented four-space markdown code, monospace/preformatted markup, distinct visual separation from the surrounding prose, or any other clear signal that this is source code rather than narrative text?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 1 uses \begin{verbatim}...\end{verbatim}. Rendered PDF shows monospaced text in a distinct block. Probe accepts any clear code-block encoding.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain the phrase 'Premature optimization is the root of all evil' (or a near-verbatim wording) attributed to Donald Knuth?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 2 quote block. Section 4 Glossary mentions Knuth and 'premature optimization'.

probe_results.question: In the document, is the Knuth quotation presented as a quotation — whether via markdown blockquote syntax (>), indentation, italics, surrounding quotation marks, attribution adjacency (e.g., 'comes from Knuth' immediately before the quote), or any other clear signal that the text is a quotation rather than the author's own prose?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 2 uses \begin{quote}...\end{quote}. Rendered PDF shows the passage indented from the surrounding paragraphs. Probe accepts any clear quotation signal.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain at least one image — whether via markdown image syntax (![alt](path)), HTML tag, embedded image reference with a path, or any other clear image-embedding signal?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 3 uses \includegraphics{chart.png}. Rendered PDF shows the bar-chart PNG. Probe accepts any clear image-embedding encoding.

probe_results.question: In the document, is there a caption associated with the figure that contains the phrase 'bar chart' or 'five vertical bars' (or equivalent wording about the chart)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 3: \caption{A sample bar chart with five vertical bars.}. Rendered PDF shows 'Figure 1: A sample bar chart with five vertical bars.' below the image.

probe_results.question: In the document, does the body text contain at least one explicit reference to a numbered figure (e.g., 'Figure 1', 'Fig. 1', or equivalent), as opposed to only describing the figure without a label?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source uses Figure~\ref{fig:chart} twice in body text. Rendered PDF shows 'Figure 1' references resolved. Probe checks whether the figure label survives in the converted body.

probe_results.question: Does the document mention that binary_search returns -1 (negative one) when no match is found?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source Section 1 body text says 'or $-1$ when no match is found' and the verbatim block ends with 'return -1'. Section 4 Glossary repeats the -1 return value.

probe_results.question: Does the document mention Bjarne Stroustrup or any other programmer's name besides Donald Knuth?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source mentions only Donald Knuth by name. Stroustrup is a thematic distractor (also a famous programming author). No other programmer names appear.

probe_results.question: In the document, does the Quotation section (about Donald Knuth) contain a code block with executable source code?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source has code only in Section 1 (Source Code). Section 2 (Quotation) contains only prose and the Knuth quote — no code. Tests whether the converter or judge invents a code block in the wrong section.
